title: MERIT Act
plain_summary: The MERIT Act says the government must offer jobs back to workers who were fired during mass layoffs between January 20, 2025 and when this law passes. These workers were still new employees on probation when they lost their jobs. The government has to give them back their old job or a similar one. The workers will also get paid for the money they lost while they were out of work. Government agencies have a set amount of time to contact these workers and give them their jobs back.
last_action_text: Read twice and referred to the Committee on Finance.

sections.title: Section 1  Short Title
sections.text: This law can be called the Model Employee Reinstatement for Ill-advised Termination Act or the MERIT Act.

sections.title: Section 2  Definitions
sections.text: In this law, these terms mean the following things. An affected probationary employee is a person who was fired or quit their federal government job as part of a large group of job cuts between January twenty, two thousand twenty five and when this law is passed. This person must have worked in the competitive service, excepted service, or Senior Executive Service in a permanent job. They also must have been serving their trial period as a new employee or had not yet worked long enough to get full job protections. Competitive service means federal jobs that people get through open competition and testing. A covered separation means either being fired from a government job except for retirement, or quitting a government job because the government offered money or other benefits to leave. Excepted service means certain federal jobs that do not require the usual competitive hiring process. Executive agency means the departments and agencies in the executive branch of the federal government. Former employing agency means the government agency where the affected employee used to work. Mass termination means at least fifteen people losing their government jobs within thirty days because of the same government actions or orders. Previous federal position means the specific job the affected employee had right before they lost it. Senior Executive Service means the highest level career positions in the federal government.

sections.title: Section 3  Reinstatement of Affected Probationary Employees
sections.text: Each affected probationary employee has the right to get their old job back or a similar job at the same government agency where they used to work. If they choose to take this job, they also get paid the money they would have earned from the day they were fired until the day they start working again. If an affected employee already got a new federal job and still has it when this law passes, they get paid the difference between what they would have earned at their old job and what they actually earned at their new job. If an affected employee got a new federal job but no longer has it when this law passes, they can get their old job back and receive both types of payments described above. However, employees do not get any payment if the amount would be less than zero. The government agency must start making these payments within ninety days after figuring out how much the employee should have been paid. All payments are made in one lump sum and are treated as regular wages for tax purposes. When hiring people back, agencies do not need to follow normal competitive hiring rules. A job is considered the same or similar to the old job only if it offers equal or better benefits including retirement, health insurance, and vacation time.

sections.title: Section 4  Notice and Selection
sections.text: Within thirty days after this law passes, each government agency must tell their former employees who qualify for job reinstatement about their rights under this law. The agency must also explain how employees can accept or reject the job offer. Employees have thirty days after getting this notice to tell their former agency whether they want the job back. If an employee does not respond within thirty days, they lose the right to get their job back. If an employee accepts the job offer, the agency must give them the job within thirty days after the employee tells them yes.

sections.title: Section 5  Separation Treatment
sections.text: Each affected probationary employee is considered to have been fired without cause from their previous federal job.

sections.title: Section 6  Payment Determination
sections.text: The Director of the Office of Personnel Management will figure out how much pay each affected employee should receive. The Director will use evidence that the employee provides about their old salary. If the employee does not provide enough evidence, the Director will use other information to determine the pay amount. Employees have up to sixty days after getting their notice or until the Director makes the pay decision to provide evidence of their salary. Government agencies must give the Director any information needed to carry out this law.

sections.title: Section 7  Reports
sections.text: Within sixty days after this law passes, the Comptroller General must give Congress a report about the mass firings that happened between January twenty, two thousand twenty five and when this law is passed. The report must include the total number of employees who were fired or quit as part of these mass firings, broken down by each government agency. For employees who were fired, the report must explain the reasons given for firing them. The report must also count how many affected probationary employees there were and make recommendations about whether other fired employees should get the same protections as this law provides. Within ninety days after this law passes, the Director of the Office of Personnel Management must give Congress a report about bringing back the affected probationary employees. This report must include how many employees were notified about their rights and how many accepted job offers under this law.
